routes.title: New York City Architecture Walk
routes.description: A walk through centuries of design.

routes.title: NYC Animals Adventure
routes.description: A playful walk through the wild side of Central Park.
routes.narratorGuidance: You are the Animal Explorer, a friendly guide for curious kids discovering the wildlife hidden inside big cities. Focus on birds, squirrels, ducks, turtles, and other animals living alongside people in parks, ponds, trees, and buildings. Keep the tone playful and observant, ask occasional questions, encourage listeners to imagine what animals are doing when humans are not around, and explain how nature survives in a busy city.

routes.title: Superheroes of NYC
routes.description: A comic-book adventure through Midtown ending at the Brooklyn Bridge, where real New York history meets the world of superheroes.
routes.narratorGuidance: You are the Superhero Guide for curious kids and preteens. Keep the delivery cinematic and exciting, but let real New York comic-book history lead each stop. When the route mentions creators like Stan Lee, Jack Kirby, Steve Ditko, or Bob Kane, treat those names as real people connected to the city's creative history rather than as background flavor.

voice.storyLens: Treat real New York as the engine that made superhero cities feel possible. Keep creators, landmarks, and visual influence specific and concrete.
voice.transitionStyle: Leave each stop by making the next location feel like the next clue in how real NYC became superhero mythology.
bannedPatterns: Do not drift into generic superhero hype without tying it back to a real New York detail.
Do not bury creator names or landmark names under vague language.

stops.title: Times Square
stops.narratorGuidance: Frame this as the public-facing spectacle of superhero New York, but keep the focus on why Midtown's energy became part of comic-book city language.

narration.angle: Use Times Square as the opening proof that real Manhattan already looked like a superhero stage before the movies.
factBullets: Times Square's giant signs, bright lights, and nonstop crowds made Midtown feel larger than life long before superhero movies used that energy on screen.
Marvel and DC stories often turn streets like this into public stages for battles, rescues, and dramatic entrances because real New York already feels theatrical.
Creators such as Stan Lee and Jack Kirby worked in a city whose spectacle made ordinary streets feel ready for extraordinary events.

stops.title: Flatiron Building
stops.narratorGuidance: Use the building's triangle shape as the visual bridge into comic-book city design, but make the real skyline influence the main point.

narration.angle: Contrast the Flatiron's real architecture with the fictional skylines it helped inspire.
factBullets: The Flatiron Building's triangle shape made it one of New York's most distinctive early skyscrapers.
Dramatic skyline shapes like this helped artists imagine fictional comic-book city skylines such as Batman's Gotham and Superman's Metropolis.
Bob Kane drew on the mood of dark, dramatic New York architecture when shaping the atmosphere that readers associate with Gotham.
sensoryTargets: the skyline angle above the street

factBullets: The Flatiron Building's triangle shape made it one of New York's most distinctive early skyscrapers.
Dramatic skyline shapes like this helped artists imagine fictional comic-book city skylines such as Batman's Gotham and Superman's Metropolis.
Bob Kane drew on the mood of dark, dramatic New York architecture when shaping the atmosphere that readers associate with Gotham.

stops.title: Grand Central Terminal
stops.narratorGuidance: Keep the wonder of secret passageways, but center the real station infrastructure that made underground comic-book worlds believable.

narration.angle: Reveal how real New York infrastructure made secret-base storytelling feel believable.
factBullets: Grand Central Terminal includes tracks, tunnels, and hidden spaces that helped make the idea of a second city under New York feel believable.
Comic-book stories use real New York infrastructure like this to imagine secret bases, escape routes, and underground worlds beneath busy streets.
A place like Grand Central makes heroes such as Daredevil feel grounded in a city with real layers above and below the sidewalk.
sensoryTargets: the hidden layers below street level

factBullets: Grand Central Terminal includes tracks, tunnels, and hidden spaces that helped make the idea of a second city under New York feel believable.
Comic-book stories use real New York infrastructure like this to imagine secret bases, escape routes, and underground worlds beneath busy streets.

stops.title: Chrysler Building
stops.narratorGuidance: Treat this as a real skyscraper that helped define the visual language of superhero Manhattan, not just a generic tower.

narration.angle: Use the Chrysler Building to show how one real skyline form became part of comic-book vertical spectacle.
factBullets: The Chrysler Building's Art Deco crown became one of the most recognizable shapes in the Manhattan skyline.
Art Deco skyscrapers helped define the vertical, gleaming New York that comic artists used for rooftop chases and high-altitude action.
When readers imagine heroes such as Spider-Man or the Fantastic Four above Manhattan, buildings like the Chrysler help sell that feeling of height and scale.
The Chrysler Building's Art Deco crown became one of the most recognizable shapes in the Manhattan skyline.

When readers imagine heroes such as Spider-Man or the Fantastic Four above Manhattan, buildings like the Chrysler help sell that feeling of height and scale.

stops.title: Midtown Skyline
stops.narratorGuidance: Use the skyline as the proof that Manhattan itself became part of superhero storytelling.

narration.angle: Treat the skyline as the moment where the whole thesis clicks: Manhattan itself became a character.
factBullets: Midtown Manhattan's stacked towers and narrow avenues created the vertical obstacle course that made Spider-Man feel inseparable from New York.
Stan Lee and Steve Ditko helped turn Manhattan itself into a superhero setting rather than a generic background city.
Teams like the Avengers feel natural in New York because the real skyline already suggests scale, danger, motion, and spectacle.

stops.title: Brooklyn Bridge
stops.narratorGuidance: End with a big finish, but ground it in why this real bridge became such a strong comic-book image.

narration.angle: Make the Brooklyn Bridge the payoff: real infrastructure on mythic scale.
factBullets: The Brooklyn Bridge was already one of New York's great engineering symbols before superheroes existed.
Because it stretches high above the East River and connects boroughs, it became a perfect visual for comic-book rescue scenes, chases, and final battles.
A landmark like the Brooklyn Bridge turns real infrastructure into mythic scale, which is exactly what superhero storytelling loves.

routes.title: Weird & Wacky NYC History
routes.description: Odd landmarks, secret corners, and the strangest stories in the city.
routes.narratorGuidance: You are the Curious Explorer, obsessed with the strangest stories hidden in cities. Focus on funny accidents, unusual buildings, secret spaces, tiny details, and surprising moments from the past rather than formal history. Keep the tone playful, curious, and full of discovery so each stop feels like a hidden weird detail most people walk right past.
